summary.pack_name: MIR go-ready slim default terms and shortcuts pack v3
summary.purpose: A cleaned, described default pack for immediate use tomorrow. It keeps the v2 operational coverage, adds concise descriptions, and records canonical/variant relationships without trying to replace the full research-driven ontology.
qa_posture: Broad coverage retained for tomorrow-use rather than aggressively pruning useful phrase variants.
Common aliases and inflections are kept but linked to a canonical phrase.
A few long phrases remain in place and are flagged for later review rather than removed now.
Descriptions are concise operational glosses, not final ontology definitions.

entries.description: Style label for music positioned outside the mainstream or outside a stricter genre center.
Song type centered on a slower pace and an expressive, lyrical or narrative focus.
Genre label associated with blues harmony, phrasing, shuffle feel, and expressive vocal or instrumental delivery.
Blues groove built on a swung or shuffle subdivision rather than a straight eighth-note feel.
Broad style label for art-music traditions; in this default pack it is mainly a cross-genre reference point.
Genre label associated with roots-based songwriting, guitar-led accompaniment, and direct narrative delivery.
Latin dance-music label associated with cumbia rhythm and related melodic or instrumental markers.
Rhythmic/style label built around the dembow pattern and related reggaeton-adjacent grooves.
Genre label for fast breakbeat-driven electronic music with strong bass emphasis.
Broad label for festival/mainstage-oriented electronic dance music and related production language.
Style label associated with acoustic instrumentation, song-centered writing, and direct storytelling.
Broad label for rap-centered music and related beat, groove, and production language.
Dance-music label built around four-on-the-floor pulse, groove repetition, and club-oriented arrangement.
Broad label for indie-associated aesthetics or production approaches rather than one fixed sound alone.
Style label associated with swing, harmonic color, improvisation, and ensemble interplay.
Jazz-associated swung groove with a shuffle feel rather than a straight subdivision.
Style label associated with urbano production and dembow-rooted rhythmic language.
Broad band-music label associated with guitar, bass, drums, and energy-driven arrangement.
Style label associated with 808 low end, crisp hi-hat programming, and modern rap production language.
Style/market label for contemporary worship music and related harmonic, vocal, and arrangement practices.

The way musical parts, sections, and entries are organized across a piece.

Main recurring section, often carrying the song's central hook or message.
Repeated line or phrase, sometimes overlapping with chorus function.
Short memorable musical or lyrical idea designed to stand out and return.

Hook figure that anchors the chorus.
Contrasting section that breaks the main cycle before a return or final section.
Short interruption or opening in the texture, beat, or arrangement.
Section where the texture or energy is stripped back before rebuild or contrast.
Increase in density, energy, or anticipation leading toward an arrival point.
Prepared rise in tension leading toward a drop, chorus, or impact point.
Present-participle form of build; indicates that tension or density is increasing.
Arrival event where energy, bass, beat, or the main impact material comes in.
Moment where a part or the beat falls away suddenly.
Closing section that winds the track down or carries it out.
Final part or finish of the piece.
A distinct formal region within the piece.
Plural form of section; multiple formal regions.

Plural form of transition; multiple connecting moves.

Main melodic idea heard near the start of the piece.

Describes material that occurs again.

Describes sound softened or blurred by ambience or smear.

Accent pattern centered on beats two and four in common time.

The first beat of a bar or a strong structural accent point.
Forward-propelling rhythmic momentum or kinetic push.
Describes music that feels strongly propelled or pushed forward.
The felt rhythmic pocket created by timing, pattern, and repeated pulse.
Describes music that sits strongly in a groove or pocket.
Present-participle form of groove; indicates active groove motion.
Plural form of groove; multiple groove figures or repeated rhythmic patterns.
Time-feel where the beat is perceived at half the apparent pulse rate.
Subdivision or accent that falls away from the main beat.
Placement that lands directly on the main beat.
Placed tightly on the timing grid with little or no intentional looseness.
The steady underlying beat felt beneath the music.
Plural form of pulse; repeated beat units or recurring beat patterns.
Playing slightly behind the beat to create drag, ease, or laid-back feel.
Forward rhythmic pressure or urgency in timing and motion.
Playing slightly ahead of the beat to create urgency or forward lean.
The patterned organization of durations, accents, and movement in time.
Describes something shaped strongly by rhythm or pulse.
Adverb form of rhythmic; indicates that something operates in a rhythmic way.
Hi-hat figure built from repeated or fast subdivisions that create rolling motion.
Snare figure built from repeated hits or rapid subdivisions to create motion.
Swung subdivision with a long-short feel rather than evenly straight division.
Uneven subdivision or timing feel that creates lilt, pocket, or bounce.
Present-participle form of swing; indicates an actively swung feel.
Describes rhythm that accents unexpected or off-beat positions.
An upbeat pickup or lighter accent leading toward a stronger beat.
Describes notes of a chord played one after another rather than all at once.
Chord tones played in sequence rather than as a block chord.
Set of notes sounded together as a harmonic unit.
Plural form of chord; multiple harmonic units.
Ordered sequence of chords across a phrase or section.
Describes something relating to harmony or chordal function.
Distance in pitch between two notes.
Plural form of interval; multiple pitch distances.

Adverb form indicating something is functioning in a melodic way.
Interval spanning eight scale degrees or a doubling at the octave.
Plural form of octave; multiple octave relationships or octave doublings.
Perceived highness or lowness of a note or sound.
Describes sound assigned to a specific pitch or tuned area.
Ordered movement through a sequence, usually harmonic.
Fast passage moving through adjacent notes of a scale.
Describes harmony with a suspended tone replacing a more stable chord tone.
The home or central pitch/chord of a key or tonal center.
Sense of expectancy created before an arrival or resolution.
Moving upward in pitch, intensity, or register.
Highest point of tension, intensity, or emotional arrival.
Noticeable difference between sections, textures, or ideas.
Gradual increase in loudness or intensity.

Moving downward in pitch, intensity, or register.

Verb phrase indicating gradual disappearance to silence.
Verb indicating the music reaches its ending point.
The moment of arrival after buildup, motion, or tension.
The sense of ongoing forward motion through the piece.
Perceived motion or progression of material through time.
